items.q: If air resistance is ignored, which falls faster: a heavy ball or a light ball dropped from the same height?
items.correct: Both fall at the same rate because gravitational acceleration is independent of mass.
items.misconception: Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects because gravity pulls more on them.
items.random_wrong: The lighter ball falls faster because it has less inertia.
items.why: Students reason 'more gravity force = more acceleration' but forget the heavier object also has proportionally more inertia, cancelling out.

items.q: Why do atoms emit light only at specific wavelengths rather than a continuous spectrum?
items.correct: Electrons occupy discrete energy levels; transitions between levels emit photons of specific frequencies (E = hf).
items.misconception: Atoms can emit any wavelength, but only certain ones are visible.
items.random_wrong: Atoms emit only one colour each, determined by their temperature.
items.why: Students don't internalise the quantisation of energy levels; without it, line spectra look arbitrary instead of a direct consequence of quantum mechanics.

items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.

items.q: A car travels 4 km north then 3 km east. What is its displacement magnitude?
items.why: Correct answer: 5 km. “7 km” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
items.why: It's Kinetic energy. “Acceleration” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
